chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, which likely passes unvalidated attacker-controlled length to memmove based on fuzzed input bytes interpreted as BSON.

Tooling could not decompile functions due to HTTP 404 errors, so exact source/dest/len expressions are unavailable. However, the function names and call sequence strongly indicate parsing of a byte[] into a BSON object without prior validation in Java or JNI, especially under fuzzing where arbitrary data is passed.

The JNI entrypoint Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes likely takes a Java byte[] and length, forwards to bson_object_from_bytes_len, then bson_array_from_bytes_len, then byte_array_to_bson_string, which eventually calls memmove. If the BSON length or string size fields are read from the attacker-controlled byte array and used directly as memmove length, this is a classic out-of-bounds read/write scenario.

Because this is part of a BSON C library embedded in an app, it is plausible that the same code path is used for real network or file inputs (e.g., meeting data), not only the fuzz harness. There is no evidence of harness-only misuse (no NULL-only crash, no impossible types), just arbitrary bytes triggering a low-level memmove crash.

Given the generic crash reason 'memmove' in a parser path that consumes untrusted bytes, it is very likely there is insufficient bounds checking around length fields or string sizes in the BSON decoder, leading to buffer overflow or out-of-bounds memory access.

Without exact code, we cannot assign a precise CWE subtype beyond generic out-of-bounds read/write, but the use of memmove with uncontrolled length derived from data suggests CWE-787 or CWE-119 class issues.

Therefore, classify as a likely genuine memory-safety vulnerability with high impact (potential RCE or at least denial-of-service) and medium-high confidence, noting that confidence is limited by missing decompilation details.
reasons: Crash occurs inside memmove called from BSON byte-array decoding path, indicating unsafe memory copy with attacker-controlled length
Call chain shows direct parsing of untrusted byte[] into native BSON structures via JNI without evidence of prior size validation
Such patterns in C parsing libraries commonly correspond to out-of-bounds read/write when length fields in the input are malformed
recommendations: Manually audit bson_*_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string implementations, ensuring all lengths derived from input are validated against actual buffer sizes before memmove/memcpy
Add robust bounds checks for BSON element and string sizes, rejecting or truncating inputs that would exceed buffer limits
Enable and run with AddressSanitizer or similar on the native library plus targeted fuzzing on BSON decoding to pinpoint and fix the exact overflow
assumptions: The BSON decoding functions use length or size fields read from the input byte array without adequate validation
The same native decoding path is reachable with real-world untrusted inputs (e.g., network or file data), not only via the fuzz harness
limitations: Ghidra MCP failed to decompile the specific functions due to tool HTTP errors, so exact source code and argument traces to memmove are unavailable
Java-side call graph and parameter validation in BsonEncoder were not directly inspected, reducing certainty about existing length checks

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed BSON byte array passed into native BSON decoder via Java JNI bridge
prerequisites: Ability to provide or influence BSON-formatted byte arrays consumed by the app (e.g., via network, file, or IPC)
Target device running a vulnerable version of com.cisco.webex.meetings with this libbson-c-lib.so
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON-like byte array with intentionally malformed length fields (e.g., large string size or array length) to cause memmove to operate beyond buffer bounds
Deliver this byte array to the vulnerable code path (e.g., through a feature that imports or syncs BSON/JSON-like data, or another app interface that wraps BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes)
Trigger the JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes so that the crafted bytes are passed down to bson_object_from_bytes_len and eventually to byte_array_to_bson_string
Observe app crash or memory corruption when memmove is invoked with the attacker-controlled length; refine payload to explore potential control of overwritten data structures
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.cisco.webex.meetings/.MainActivity --es bson_file /sdcard/Download/payload.bson # assumes an activity or path that loads the file and calls into BsonEncoder
exploit.notes: Exact app entrypoint that passes external data into BsonEncoder is unknown; PoC assumes there is some feature reading BSON-like data from storage or network and routing it through this JNI bridge.

chain_of_thought: Native crash is in memmove called by byte_array_to_bson_string → bson_object_from_bytes_len → JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes. memmove crash under fuzzing usually indicates out-of-bounds read/write or overlap issues. However MCP search for the specific functions failed (404), so I can’t inspect implementation or argument flow. I also lack Java callgraph or JNI argument details beyond the bridge name. I must still decide if this is likely a real vulnerability vs harness-only issue.

Given the function names and library (libbson-c-lib.so) this is a BSON encoder/decoder implementation. byte_array_to_bson_string and bson_object_from_bytes_len clearly act on attacker-influenced byte arrays coming from Java Byte[] / byte[] inputs. In realistic app use, BSON payloads are derived from network or IPC data, so this is plausibly attacker-controlled. There’s no indication of the harness doing something impossible (like passing null where app never would); fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input just feeds arbitrary bytes like untrusted data.

The crash reason given is just `Process Termination : memmove` without allocator abort context, but in native code, unbounded memmove using user-derived length can cause OOB access, leading to crash. With no code we can’t see validations. However, function naming suggests a typical pattern: bson_object_from_bytes_len probably takes (uint8_t *bytes, size_t len) and passes it to byte_array_to_bson_string, which may assume the bytes encode a valid string (e.g., length prefix or null-terminated). If length is taken from attacker-controlled data or length field in BSON without sanity checking, memmove may copy beyond buffer bounds. This is plausible and consistent with a fuzz-found crash.

Key question: is this just a benign robustness DoS (parser rejecting malformed BSON via abort) or evidence of unsafe memory handling? Since the termination is directly at memmove, not via explicit sanity check or explicit abort call, the likely mechanism is invalid pointer or size passed to memmove causing crash. That’s a memory safety bug, even if only DoS; it’s still a vulnerability by the given definition (unsafe behaviour, potential denial-of-service, possibly exploitable).

We must weigh: lack of direct decompile evidence (due to tool errors) vs strong semantic hints. Fuzzer found a native crash in a parser-like function called from a JNI bridge that processes external data. memmove is a classic primitive that requires careful bounds checks. In mature code, malformed BSON should be handled by length checks and error returns, not by segfault in memmove. Therefore this is likely a real bug, though exploitability (code execution) is unclear. I will classify as vulnerable with medium severity and moderate confidence (~0.7). CWE likely CWE-787 (OOB write) or CWE-125 (OOB read); since memmove can corrupt memory I lean CWE-787, but exact direction is unknown.

For recommendations, I’ll suggest auditing byte_array_to_bson_string and bson_object_from_bytes_len, adding robust bounds checking for lengths and ensuring memmove length/source/dest are validated against buffer sizes. For exploit field, I’ll assume attacker can feed crafted BSON data via app’s BSON-related features (e.g., meetings payloads) and give generic adb-based PoC guidance with placeholders, making assumptions explicit.
reasons: Crash occurs in memmove invoked by BSON parsing functions, indicating unsafe memory copy on malformed input

Crash is directly in library code (libbson-c-lib.so), not obviously due to fuzz harness misusing JNI (normal Java code can pass arbitrary byte[] of varying lengths)
Lack of explicit error handling or abort frame suggests raw memmove received invalid pointer/length, implying an out-of-bounds memory access
recommendations: Manually audit byte_array_to_bson_string and bson_object_from_bytes_len for all memmove/memcpy calls and enforce strict bounds checks on lengths and indices derived from BSON data
Add robust validation of BSON length fields and string sizes before performing any memory moves; reject or truncate malformed inputs rather than proceeding
Harden JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes to validate byte[] length and catch native errors, and add fuzz tests around the fixed implementation

The fuzz harness calls the JNI bridge in a way that is representative of real app use (passing arbitrary but well-typed byte[])
.so binary semantics (BSON handling functions and memmove usage) follow conventional patterns inferred from names

No detailed crash context (registers, precise fault address, signal) to distinguish out-of-bounds read vs write or null dereference
No Java callgraph available to confirm exact sources of input bytes and their validation status

exploit.trigger_method: Supply crafted BSON byte[] to code path invoking BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the application to parse attacker-controlled BSON data via network, file, or IPC
The vulnerable code path using Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is reachable in production builds
No additional mitigations (e.g., strict sandbox, hardened allocator) fully prevent process termination
exploit_pipeline: Identify an app feature that accepts or constructs BSON payloads and passes them to the BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes JNI bridge
Craft a BSON byte sequence with malformed length fields or string encodings designed to cause excessively large or unaligned memmove lengths
Deliver the crafted BSON payload to the target feature (e.g., via network message, imported meeting data, or IPC) so that it is passed into the native parser
Trigger the parsing operation to cause byte_array_to_bson_string to call memmove with invalid parameters, leading to native crash and denial-of-service

exploit.notes: Exploitability beyond denial-of-service (e.g., code execution) depends on the precise nature of the memmove misuse, which could not be confirmed without decompilation. Fuzzing should be extended with address/undefined behavior sanitizers to better characterize the memory corruption.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove (allocator/libc); first app-level frame below is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so.
Crash type is a memmove termination, which usually indicates out-of-bounds read/write or overlapping/invalid pointers. The stack shows a BSON parsing/decoding path from raw bytes to a BSON object.

Because Ghidra search failed (404) I cannot see the exact implementation, but function naming and typical BSON libraries suggest: Java passes a byte[] (and maybe length); JNI wrapper forwards pointer and length to bson_object_from_bytes_len, which then calls byte_array_to_bson_string, which calls memmove/memcpy on internal buffers sized based on parsed length fields in the input.

The harness entry is fuzz_one_input feeding arbitrary bytes; in a real app, these bytes would come from network, files, or IPC (Webex meetings exchanging BSON data). Therefore the input is realistically attacker-controlled and the same JNI path is used in production (Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes).

Given there is a direct crash in memmove during BSON decoding with attacker-controlled byte content and length fields, the most plausible explanation is missing or insufficient bounds checking on lengths or offsets derived from the untrusted BSON, leading to out-of-bounds memory access or invalid pointer in memmove.

There is no evidence that this is a harness-only misuse (e.g., passing NULL pointers or impossible sizes); the JNI method itself is part of the shipping library and named like a normal encoder/decoder utility. Fuzzer simply exposes the underlying bug earlier.

Thus this is likely a genuine memory safety vulnerability (CWE-787/119 style) with high impact: attacker can cause crashes (DoS) and probably control read/write size to some extent. Without the exact code we cannot assert full RCE, but a high-severity memory corruption is reasonable.

Confidence is not 1.0 because we lack direct decompiled code, but the crash pattern, symbol names, and dataflow (untrusted bytes -> BSON decode -> memmove) strongly support a real bug rather than environmental artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs inside memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string while decoding attacker-controlled BSON bytes
JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is a real app API used for BSON parsing, not a fuzz-only stub
Typical failure mode for such a stack is out-of-bounds or invalid-pointer memmove due to insufficient bounds checking on lengths derived from untrusted data
No indicators that the crash results purely from impossible harness conditions like forced NULL pointers
recommendations: Manually review byte_array_to_bson_string and bson_object_from_bytes_len implementations for all memmove/memcpy/memset calls and ensure destination buffers are sized and bounds-checked against computed lengths
Add strict validation of BSON length fields and object sizes before allocating/copying, rejecting malformed or excessively large inputs early
Compile native libraries with full ASan/UBSan and run dedicated fuzzing on all BSON decode/encode JNI methods to confirm the fix and catch similar issues

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed attacker-controlled BSON payload passed into the BSON decoder JNI API
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to decode arbitrary BSON bytes (e.g., via network messages, files, or IPC)
Target device runs a vulnerable version of com.cisco.webex.meetings with the affected libbson-c-lib.so
exploit_pipeline: Craft a BSON-like byte sequence with malformed or extreme length fields designed to cause out-of-bounds copying during BSON string decoding
Deliver the crafted payload to the Webex Meetings app over a vector that is parsed into a BSON object (e.g., meeting data, chat, or configuration sync)
Ensure the app calls into Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes with the attacker-controlled bytes
When the decoder processes the payload, memmove in byte_array_to_bson_string is invoked with unsafe parameters, corrupting memory and crashing the process
Iterate on payload structure and lengths to explore controlled memory corruption and potential code execution

exploit.notes: Precise payload structure and triggering component depend on the specific Java callgraph and app logic that reach BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes; these must be identified by reverse engineering the APK and tracing calls to the JNI method.

chain_of_thought: Tooling failed with 404 on direct function-name search, but we still must assess from the structured crash info and library map.
The native stack shows crash in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string → bson_object_from_bytes_len → bson_array_from_bytes_len → bson_object_from_bytes_len → JNI bridge.
memmove abort/termination during fuzzing strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap issue on a buffer derived from attacker-controlled bytes (fuzzer input / Java byte[]).

The function names (byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_*_from_bytes_len) clearly indicate parsing BSON objects/arrays from a raw byte buffer of given length; fuzzing harness passes arbitrary bytes, mimicking untrusted input in real usage (network, IPC, files).
memmove itself is the allocator/library function; first app-level frame is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so → this is where memory copy/length logic likely resides.

Although we cannot decompile due to MCP errors, the signature pattern is standard: converting a raw byte array into an internal BSON string; incorrect length or bounds checking easily causes memmove to operate beyond source/destination buffers.

The name bson_*_from_bytes_len indicates the API takes a pointer + length; if any of these functions blindly trusts a length derived from the supplied buffer or miscalculates a field length inside the buffer, copying into fixed or smaller buffers using memmove can overflow.

Because the crash is at memmove and there is no evidence of harness doing obviously invalid JNI usage (no NULL in trace, and JNI function is a real app-exported symbol), this is unlikely to be a harness-only bug; it is an unsafe parsing routine reachable via regular JNI.

Attack surface: Java bridge method BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes likely receives a byte[] from app code; in real app this byte[] could be directly influenced by remote Webex data or external inputs without extra native-side checking, so attacker control is realistic.

Given typical BSON parsers, mis-parsing length fields or nested array/object lengths is a well-known class of memory safety bugs; here, memmove termination is consistent with out-of-bounds, and no strong evidence of prior bounds checks or defensive coding is visible.

Therefore classify as a likely memory-corruption vulnerability (out-of-bounds read/write via memmove) with high severity; confidence not maximal due to missing decompiled code but strong from crash pattern and function naming.

The malformed lengths cause memmove in byte_array_to_bson_string to operate out of bounds, corrupting heap memory or triggering a crash
